coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on dual-emissive carbon dot for the selective detection of Cd2+
coredata.dc:creator: Keerthana, P.
Kumar Das, Avijit
Bharath, M.
Ghosh, Munmun
Varghese, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium (Cd2+), a heavy metal ion used in numerous industries, has toxic adverse effects on the environment; it is crucial to develop a quick and reliable method for Cd2+ determination. Fluorescent biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CD) with rich carboxyl groups on the surface were synthesized using water amaranth leaves by hydrothermal method with a 12.1% quantum yield. The surface of CD was further modified with 1-pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC) to synthesize pyrene carboxaldehyde-carbon quantum dots (PC-CD). This study developed a fluorescent ratiometric nanosensor using a covalently functionalized CD with pyrene derivative and demonstrates highly selective identification capability towards Cd2+ over competing metal ions. The Nano sensor has significant selectivity towards Cd2+ in an excellent linear range of 0–70 µM with a detection limit as low as 15 nM and demonstrates excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) were used to identify the surface functionalization of PC-CD. Finally, the developed ratiometric sensor was used for detecting Cd2+ metal ions from various water effluents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Biomass-derived
Carbon quantum dots
Cadmium
Pollutant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of CD, PC, and PC-CD
Fluorescence detection of Cd2+ using PC-W-CQD
Optimization of PC-CD and PC-CD for Cd2+ sensing
Selectivity study for the detection of Cd2+ ion
Quenching mechanism of PC-CD for Cd2+
Determination of Cd2+ ion in various water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the centre for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of CD, (b) Raman spectra of CD, (c) AFM image of the CD. aggregation property and diameter of CD were determined using AFM. The diameter was found to be in the 6–10 nm range.
TEM images of (a) CDs, (d) PC-CDs; HRTEM images of (b) CDs, (e) PC-CDs and; Size distribution histograms of (c) CDs, (f) PC-CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of CD, PC, and PC-CD.
XPS spectra (a) survey spectra of PC-CD (b) C 1 s spectra of PC- CD (c) O 1 s spectra of PC-CD (d) N 1 s spectra of PC-CD.

(a) UV-Vis absorption spectra of CD, PC, PC-CD and PC-CD+Cd2+ (b) Fluorescence spectra of CD, PC, PC-CD and PC-CD+Cd2+ (c) CDs excitation dependent emission spectra (d) CDs area normalised excitation dependent emission spectra (e) Zeta potential of CD, PC, PC-CD.

(a) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra of PC-CD after the addition of Cd2+ (0–70 μM) in the presence of PBS solution (b) Linear plot between the various concentration of Cd2+ ion (0–70 μM) and PL intensity ratio (I666/I476) (c) PC-CDs CIE chromaticity coordinates after the addition of Cd2+ (d) Linear plot of R/G value against the concentration of Cd2+ (e) Presence of various concentration of Cd2+(0–70 μM) under visible light and UV light @ 365 nm.
(a) Effect of time on fluorescence intensity of PC-CD (b)Effect pH on fluorescence intensity of PC-CD in the absence and presence of Cd2+ ion.

(a) Selectivity of Cd2+ detection towards other metal ions. (b) anti-interference experiment of PC-CDs towards different ions in the presence/absence of Cd (II) (c) The RGB relative ratios of the fluorescent pictures in the presence of various analytes. (d) Photograph of various analytes in PC-CD under UV light.
(a) Lifetime study OD PC-CD in the presence and absence of Cd2+ (b) PC-CD + Cd2+ Stern-Volmer plot (c) PC-CD + Cd2+ Modified Stern-Volmer plot.
Cell viability assay of PC-CD and PC-CD+Cd2+ after 24 hrs.
: Synthesis of biomass derived carbon quantum dots (CD).
Comparison table of proposed ratiometric work with the other reported literature on Cd2+ determination.

row.entry: Nitrogen, phosphorous codoped carbon quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of Cd2+ ion using various industrial water effluents.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on dual-emissive carbon dot for the selective detection of Cd2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ashoka University, Haryana 131029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Ashoka University, Haryana-131029, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium (Cd2+), a heavy metal ion used in numerous industries, has toxic adverse effects on the environment; it is crucial to develop a quick and reliable method for Cd2+ determination. Fluorescent biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CD) with rich carboxyl groups on the surface were synthesized using water amaranth leaves by hydrothermal method with a 12.1% quantum yield. The surface of CD was further modified with 1-pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC) to synthesize pyrene carboxaldehyde-carbon quantum dots (PC-CD). This study developed a fluorescent ratiometric nanosensor using a covalently functionalized CD with pyrene derivative and demonstrates highly selective identification capability towards Cd2+ over competing metal ions. The Nano sensor has significant selectivity towards Cd2+ in an excellent linear range of 0–70 µM with a detection limit as low as 15 nM and demonstrates excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) were used to identify the surface functionalization of PC-CD. Finally, the developed ratiometric sensor was used for detecting Cd2+ metal ions from various water effluents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions like lead (Pb), cadmium (Cd), and mercury (Hg) are exceptionally harmful pollutants retained in the environment as contaminants in food and drinking water [1–3]. In past years, widespread attention by world nations in monitoring harmful components in drinking water and food led to findings that these toxic metal ions prompt malignant tumor growth in human beings, particularly infants [4–6]. Cadmium is considered one of the strongly accumulated toxic metal ions in living organisms. Much emphasis on cadmium poisoning and its adverse effects was noticed after the “itai” sickness (mass Cd2+ harming) outburst in Japan (1955) [7]. The cadmium (II) level (Cd2+) in the environment is enhanced because of the expanding chemical industries and human exercises. Their major elimination to the environment happens during the processes like zinc refining, battery manufacturing, plastic production, and metal coating industries [8–10]. According to the study by The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA-USA) and International World Health Organization (WHO), the threshold limit of Cd2+ ions in drinking water is 5ppb [11,12]. Not just in human beings, widely varied flora and fauna endure a great deal from Cd2+ poisoning [13,14]. Also, natural resources like composts, mud, lime, and excrements containing harmful metal ions result in the aggregation of toxic metal ions in the plant body resulting in their improper functioning like producing harmed seeds, obstructing the transportation of polynucleotides [15] and enzymes, cooking of root tips, denaturing proteins, affecting photosynthesis [16,17], upsetting the safeguard capacity of cell reinforcements, putrefaction, chlorosis, and passing of tissues [18,19]. Thus, the identification and removal of Cd2+ ions from water and food resources need utmost importance [20].

Many analytical and electro-analytical techniques like absorption spectroscopy, liquid chromatography, cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, ICP-MS, and ICP-AES were widely employed earlier, for the detection of heavy metal ions [21–25]. Despite the ease of application, their practicality is constrained due to poor selectivity and high cost. Last few years fluorescent sensing technology was introduced for sensing heavy metal ions as they offer high sensitivity, fast kinetics, easy functionalization, economic feasibility, and high potential. Likely, certain chromophores and their derivatives like BODIPY, rhodamine, quantum dots, and fluorescein were used for detecting analytes [26–28].

Among them, biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (BCDs) were excellent fluorescent nanomaterials. Compared to other carbon sources, biomass carbon sources are eco-friendly that have several benefits for creating BCDs, including being cheap, accessible, abundant, and green. The generation of BCDs from natural biomass can transform waste biomass of poor value into value-added and practical materials. Contrary to CDs from chemical carbon sources, which demand the addition of external heteroatoms, biomass-containing heteroatoms are the suitable raw material for the formation of CDs and wide applications such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and analyte detection. Recently, Varsha et.al., synthesized biomass derived carbon quantum dot using mint leaves for the detection of Fe (III) and ascorbic acid with a detection limit of 374 nM and 79 nM, respectively [29]. Kalanidhi et.al., synthesized fluorescent N-doped biomass carbon dots from betel leaves for sensitive detection of Picric acid and Iron ion with a detection limit of 0.11 μM and 0.135 μM respectively [30]. But they still lack wide applicability due to low quantum yield and a fewer number of active sites. There are many reports available for surface functionalized CQDs showing better activity for widespread applications [31–34].

Most of the fluorescence responses to detect Cd2+, are based on a single quenching fluorescence probe, mainly compromised by factors such as changes in sensor concentration, light source or detector drift, or environmental effects in complex samples. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors have recently received much attention, which can avoid these issues. The ratiometric fluorescence probe's signal variation is easier to recognize with the naked eye. The intensity ratio of two well-defined wavelengths provides built-in correction for environmental interference and eliminates excitation light intensity fluctuation, improving quantification accuracy [35–38]. Fanyong Yan et.al. synthesize carbon quantum dots, surface functionalized by fluorescein derivative, and used them as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect hypochlorite with a detection limit of 93 nM [39]. Zhaomin zhang et.al. synthesize functionalized carbon dots using quinoline derivative as a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for detecting Zn2+ with 6.4 nM as a detection limit [40]. Qianghua Ye et.al. synthesize carbon dots and functionalized them using 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid to detect catechol with a detection limit of 115 nM [41] . CQDs functionalized with organic fluorophores with a form of surface passivation can improve fluorescence capabilities to detect Cd2+. It acts as an effective ratiometric sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Cd2+.

In this work, we synthesized a novel ratiometric dual emission fluorescence probe for the detection of Cd2+. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor development is based on biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CDs) synthesized in a hydrothermal method followed by surface functionalization with Pyrene derivative in a reflux condition (PC-CD) ( Scheme 1). The developed PC-CD could attain better photoluminescence properties and act as an effective ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the highly selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ industrial effluents.

Leaves of Water amaranth were collected from a local garden, in Tamilnadu, India. Chlorides of metals Ag+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cs+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Pb2+ were procured from Sigma Aldrich., India. Ethanol and carbonates of Cadmium metal were obtained from SD-Fine chem Pvt Ltd., India. Metal nitrate salt (Pb2+) was obtained from Qualigens., India. All the chemicals were of analytical grade. Metal ions solutions were prepared for the experiment using double-distilled water.

Thermo Nicolet, Avatar 370, was used to identify the quantum dots’ and modified quantum dots surface group functionalities using an FT-IR spectrum (instrument spec). TEM images were captured using a JEOL (JEM-F200) electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Powder XRD patterns were determined using a Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer and Cu K radiation (= 1.5406). The Raman spectrum was recorded using the Lab RAM HR FT-Raman module at 532 nm LASER. The XPS spectrum was recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology). For absorption measurements, (UV–visible 1800) a Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer was used. Shimadzu spectrofluorometer was used for all photoluminescence (PL) measurements (emission, excitation, contour map, and PL) (RF5301PC). PBS at pH 7 medium was used to record all measurements.

A green, one-step process was followed to synthesize the CD from Water amaranth leaves. The leaves were collected, washed, sun-dried, grounded, and suspended in water: ethanol (50:50, 100 mL) solution. The CDs were fabricated by a one-pot hydrothermal process, the suspended solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 5 h (Scheme 1). The obtained brown solution was centrifuged (15000 rpm, 25 min) to remove the insoluble particles. The final product was subjected to dialysis to remove any unreacted molecules or partial carbonization products. The obtained solution was stored under 4 °C and used as it is for further studies. Tables.S1, S2, and S3 explained the optimization technique for the synthesis of CD.

Pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC) (500 μg) and CD (500 μg) were dissolved in 30 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stirred at 600 C for 24 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the reaction mixture was centrifuged (25000 rpm, 10 min) to remove unreacted particles, followed by dialysis for 48 h. After the removal of the solvent by a rotary evaporator, the product was purified using column chromatography (methanol/methylene chloride v/v 1:5). The obtained nanoparticles were dried and used for further studies. Table.S4 explained the optimization technique for the synthesis of PC-CD.

Different concentration of analytes was mixed with 10 μl phosphate buffer solution and 20 μl PC-CD/ethanol to test the selectivity. To test the sensitivity, 20 μl of PC-CD/ethanol was mixed with various concentrations of Cd2+ solutions to make a 10 mL clear solution. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS), pH= 7.4, was used for the detection of Cd2+ and PL spectra were captured at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm (5/5 nm slit width). Fluorescence on-site measurement RGB values for the detection of Cd2+ ion were measured using a smartphone app (RGB detector) under UV light @ 365 nm.

Cytotoxicity was assessed using the HeLa cell line, with a suitable culture protocol. HeLa cells were seeded in a culture medium (96-well plate), having a density of 1 × 105 cells per well, and incubated with MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl) 2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. At the end of the incubation period, PBS-prepared MTT solution was added to each well plate and kept for 3 h incubation. DMSO was added to the culture medium to dissolve formazan crystals after taking out the MTT solution. Formazan crystals were observed under 570 nm excitation wavelength and the absorbance value is used to calculate the cell viability percentage.
(1) % of cell viability = Treated cells absorbance / controlled cells absorbance

The efficacy of PC-CD as a fluorescent sensor is studied by detecting Cd2+ ions in various industrial water effluents, under precisely controlled parameters. Water samples from different sources were collected, filtered and used. Samples were then left to stand for two days under 4 °C. To assess the PC-CD ratiometric sensor recovery capability, Cd2+ spiked water samples were also examined.

The formation of CD was characterized using XRD and Raman Spectroscopy (
Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 a shows the XRD pattern of CD. A broad single peak at 2Ɵ = 25.5 corresponds to the carbon dots with the sp3 domain. Also, the noisy behavior of the peak explains the amorphous nature of CD. Raman spectra of CD showed two peaks at 1371 and 1584 cm−1 (Fig. 1 b), attributed to G and D bands, respectively. D band clarifies the carbon atom movement with dangling bonds in the plane owing to glassy carbon or graphite disordered structure (A1 g). The graphitic peak associated with the C-C stretching vibration-linked atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) lattice is explained by the G band (E2 g mode). The amount of B/N-CQD defect was assessed by analyzing the peak location and intensity of D and G bands (ID/IG). The computed ID/IG ratio is found to be 1.21, corresponding to the sp3/sp2 carbon domain's typical disorder extent [42]. To further investigate the surface characteristics, AFM analysis of CD was recorded (Fig. 1 c). The.

TEM characterization confirmed the quasi-spherical shape of CD and PC-CD ( Fig. 2 a-e). From Fig. 2 a-b it can be presumed that CD has a restricted size distribution in the 6–7.5 nm region, and its average diameter is 6.71 nm. Despite this, Fig. 2 d-e PC-CD has a size distribution in the 6.5–9 nm region with an average diameter of 7.75 nm. The histogram in Figs. 2 c and 2 f shows the average particle size distribution for over 100 nanoparticles. To further confirm the formation of PC-CD, FTIR spectra of CD, pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC), and PC-CD were analyzed ( Fig. 3).

As shown in Fig. 3, CD and PC-CD exhibit a broad adsorption band at 3337 cm−1 corresponding to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The adsorption bands near 2973 cm−1 and 2884 cm−1 correspond to CH3 and CH2 stretching vibrations and the peaks at 1454 cm−1 and 1388 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric and symmetric C-H bending vibrations, respectively. The bands at 1680 cm−1 are due to the carbonyl group (CO) present on the surface of the CD. Other evidence for the carbonyl group at 1043 cm−1 and 1316 cm−1 attributed to C-O and COO- stretching vibration respectively can also be seen. The bands at 1647 cm−1 indicated the presence of CC stretching vibration. The 880 cm−1 band corresponds to the C-H bending vibration. PC-CD also possesses PC bands with slight shifts, the bands at 1720 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching vibrations and the bands at 1585 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 can be assigned to CC aromatic stretching vibrations respectively. These results demonstrate the functionalization of PC over CD [43–45].

To determine the overall chemical composition and binding energy, XPS of PC-CD were analyzed. The XPS spectra showed that PC-CD comprises elements carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen and it shows C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s peaks at 284.92, 532.26, and 400.01 eV( Fig. 4) respectively. The elemental percentage composition of C, N, and O were 80.64%, 2.23%, and 15.57% (Fig. 4 a). The C 1 s XPS core level spectrum shows seven characteristic peaks as shown in Fig. 4 b. The intense high peak attributes to the C-C bond at 284.8 eV, and the peak at 284.32 eV corresponds to the CC bond. The weak intense peak observed at 289.10 eV can be assigned to the CO group confirming the presence of carbonyl groups in PC-CD and the band present.

at 286.1 eV represent the C-O bond. A significantly less intense peak was observed at 287.8 and 285.7 eV corresponding to C-N and CN bonds respectively. The broad sp2 carbon domain spectrum revealed a peak at 286.9 eV (π- π * satellite). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 4 c has four deconvoluted peaks at 531.68, 532.34, 533.28, and 534.01 eV. The broad peak at 531.68 eV attributes to the carboxyl group (CO bond) present on the PC-CDs surface. The sharp peak at 532.34 eV represents the C-O bond. The peak that appeared at 533.28 eV corresponds to hydroxyl or carboxyl groups (C-O-H/C-O-C) of PC-CD. The surface of PC-CD confirms the presence of carboxyl moiety from the peak at 534.01 eV. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4 d) displayed three distinct peaks, indicating CN, C-N, and N-H [46,47]. The C-N bond is represented by the sharp intensity peak at 399.7 eV, while the peak at 401.9 eV represents the N-H bond. The other at 400.5 eV confirms the CN bond. XPS and FTIR spectral data indicate the successful linking of PC over CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CD, PC, and PC-CD
ce:section.ce:para: We have also studied the UV-Vis absorption spectra of CD, PC, PC-CD, and PC-CD + Cd2+ ions ( Fig. 5 a). CD exhibits an absorption peak at 268 and 412 nm corresponding to π-π * transition of CC and n -π * transition of CO, respectively. PC possesses an absorption peak at 250, 282, 358, and 394 nm corresponding to the pyrene derivative (π-π * transitions). The PC-CD exhibits a slight shift in the absorption peaks at 264, 320, 387, 408, and 610 nm. After the addition of Cd2+ ions into the PC-CD solution, a bathochromic shift was observed. The disappearance of the peak occurred at 408 nm and a new peak at 427 nm appeared (inset CD absorption peak). Fig. 5 b explains the fluorescence intensity of CD, PC, PC-CD, and PC-CD + Cd2+. When CD is excited at 610 nm, it shows emission at a wavelength of 670 nm. PC shows an intense emission wavelength at 476 nm and a weak emission band centered at 377, 400 nm. The surface functionalized CD with PC shows a major emission wavelength at 476, 666 nm and a weak emission band at 377,400 nm. After the addition of the Cd2+ ion, the peak at 666 nm got enhanced while the other peaks at 377, 400, and 476 nm got quenched, confirming the fluorescence sensor's ratiometric performance. This experiment evinced that, it is theoretically possible to make a FRET nanoprobe using the CDs moiety as an energy acceptor and PC as an energy donor.

The excitation-dependent emission spectrum is seen in the synthesized CDs PL spectroscopy (Fig. 5 c; inset: CD under UV light). Due to the sizeable particles of CDs, the PL intensity showed a bathochromic shift from 530 nm to 610 nm and showed a higher emission at 610 nm and it declined from 630 nm. At 610 nm, an excitation spectrum of CD was observed. A linear relationship exists between PL intensity and the number of big particles stimulated at a given wavelength. (Fig. 5 d) explained the excitation-area normalized spectrum. We can see a bathochromic shift of PL maxima concerning the excitation wavelength after normalizing the PL spectra of CDs recorded at various excitation wavelengths is observed. Fig. S1 explains the CDs’ PL quantum yield was found to be 12.1%. The slope approach method was used to calculate the quantum yield of CD. A reference for quantum dots used was rhodamine (1.00% in ethanol). Surface functional groups are mainly responsible for the high percentage of quantum yield [48]. Fig. 5 e explains the zeta potentials of CD and PC were measured before and after the functionalization. Before functionalization, the zeta potential for CD and PC are − 43.7 mV and − 32.2 mV, respectively and after it was − 36.4 mV for PC-CD, the increment is due to the formation of an amide bond as shown in fig.S2 [49]. The zeta potential explains the surface functional groups of CD, PC, and PC-CD which correlates with the optical properties (π-π * transition of CC and n -π * transition of CO) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cd2+ using PC-W-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: In order to create a dual emission ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC) was chosen as the reference unit serving as an integrated calibration to remove background interferences and environmental influences for the following reasons: (i)There is no overlap in the fluorescence spectra of CD and pyrene carboxaldehyde, which is essential for precise ratiometric detection, particularly at low analyte concentration. It produces a huge stoke shift (> 200 nm) between the two peaks. One peak appeared at 476 nm and another peak at 666 nm. (ii) the CD and PC fluorescence can be excited by the same excitation source. A fluorescence titration method for Cd2+ was used to determine the concentration of Cd2+ in water samples. As can be seen from Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs moiety (λem =666 nm) tends to increase while the new emission peak from the PC units (λem =476 nm) exhibits a decreased tendency (inset: shows the photograph of various concentration of Cd2+ (0–70 μM) in PC-CD, identified using a UV chip in the presence of visible and UV light). An excellent correlation coefficient (R2 =0.988) indicates an amicable linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio I666/I476 and the increase in Cd2+ ion concentration from 0 to 70 μM and the regression equation found as I666/I476 = 0.2363 (Cd2+) + 0.00481 (Fig. 6 b).

The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 15 nM based on the signal-to-noise ratio which confirms the dual-emission fluorescent ratiometric nanoprobe. The concentration of Cd2+ ranges from 0–70 μM, while the CIE color coordinates of PC-CD range from blue to red (Fig. 6 c). When exposed to visible light, the PC-CD solution turns brown, whereas when exposed to UV light, it turns blue to red. The corresponding colour changes are further verified by computing the chromaticity coordinates based on the relevant fluorescence spectra using the CIE 1931 (x, y) chromaticity diagram. According to Fig. 6 c and Table S5, striking red-

shift tendency in the CIE coordinates is seen with an increase in Cd2+ concentration, giving PC-CDs a lot of potential as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for selective detection of cd2+ in resource-constrained environments. Fig. 6 d explains the on-site fluorescence detection of Cd2+ using a smartphone app and the RGB values were acquired. This technique was utilized to acquire fluorescence standard pictures, and RGB values, under various concentrations of Cd2+(0–70 μM). The ratio that considers the G and R values of the RGB values, which can show the fluorescent color change, showed a direct linear relationship in the range of 0–70 μM with a LOD of 30 nM (Fig. 6d), and the regression equation found as 0.0123 (Cd2+) + 2.899E−4 with a correlation coefficient of 0.99669. The increasing concentration of Cd2+ showed a color change gradually and it's visible under UV light (Fig. 6 e). This technique could be utilized for the visual exposure of Cd2+ because of fluorescence color changes. Scheme2 explains the surface functionalization of PC with CD and PC-CD+ Cd2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of PC-CD and PC-CD for Cd2+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the prepared PC-CDs was examined at various pH levels and in solutions with diverse ionic strengths by measuring its fluorescence intensity. Investigations were also done on the photoirradiation method and storage stability technique. As shown in Fig. S3 (a-c), there was no difference in the PL intensity of the PC-CDs when the ionic strength (0–2 M) of KCl solutions was changed. The optimal storage ability of PC-CDs was found to be around 60 days. The effect of temperatures on the stability of PC-CDs was studied in the range of 0–40ºC. There.

were no changes observed after the different days, temperature and various concentrations of KCl. An optimised reaction process for detecting Cd2+ was studied to achieve better performance. Fig. 7 a shows that the solution of PC-CD in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) exhibits good fluorescence stability. The ratio of I666/I476 increases substantially and reaches its maximum after adding various concentrations of Cd2+ to the PC-CDs solution, and this peak occurs after around one hour and is maintained for two hours. The PC-CDs solution requires a slightly longer reaction time than the previously reported Cd2+ ion-detecting probes. The less amount of PC being grafted onto the CDs improves the reaction time. The surface functionalization of CDs is modulated with a PC in this work.

Additionally, the pH range of 3.0–12.0 was studied to determine the best pH range for Cd2+ detection. As shown in Fig. 7 b, no noticeable fluorescence change was observed when the pH was changed from 3.0 to 9.0 in the absence and presence of the Cd2+ ion, which can be attributed to the CD's moiety's high degree of pH stability. The fluorescence intensity ratio increases with the addition of Cd2+ over an extensive pH range, from 3 to 12, and the pH values increased at these levels are very distinct [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study for the detection of Cd2+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of closely related compounds on the detection of Cd2+ metal ion utilizing PC-CD has been studied to evaluate the efficacy of the suggested technology, for which 400 μl of 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) and 100 μl of PC-CD were mixed at room temperature. Later, 2.5 mL of deionized water was used to dilute a mixture of various metal ions commonly linked with 2 μl concentrations of metal ions that are used to obtain a 16 μM concentration. The metal ions such as Mn2+, Cr3+, Cs+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Cd2+ were among the substances utilized for the selectivity investigation. Fig. 8 a shows how the addition of Cd2+ over other heavy metal ions to PC-CDs proves the selective fluorescence quenching intensity.

Selectivity study is a significant factor for determining the performance of chemical sensors. The Selectivity of PC-CDs towards Cd (II) and other interfering ions was thoroughly examined, including the anions and cations of I-, F-, S2O8 2-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, Cl-, Br - Mn2+, Cr3+, Cs+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Cd2+. Only the presence of Cd (II) was found to significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity ratio (I666/I476), while the presence of other ions did not affect the fluorescence ratio (Fig. 8 b), even when the analyte concentration was five times higher than that of Cd (II). Fig. 8 c explains the fluorescence on-site measurement using the ratio of R/G value. A smartphone app was used to detect the fluorescence on-site measurement of various metal ions and the RGB values were collected, as shown in Fig. 8 c. This method was used to capture RGB values and fluorescence standard images of various metal ions. The ratio that can exhibit a change in fluorescent colour while taking the G and R values of the RGB values. Many metal ions not interfere with the fluorescence intensity ratio (R/G), only the Cd2+ ion increases the CD ratio while PC decreases the emission intensity which proves the dual emission ratiometric fluorescence response mode. Fig. 8 d shown the fluorescence image of different metal ions immersed in Whatman paper (radius 2 cm) containing 100 μl of PC-CD solution, 400 μl of phosphate buffer solution, 2.5 mL of deionized water, 2 μl concentrations of metal ions, and a photograph taken under UV light and confirmed the Cd2+ ion gave red emission fluorescence response. Hence the synthesized PC-CDs showed a good selective response to Cd2+ as a dual-emission ratiometric nanoprobe. Table 1 explains the proposed work with reported literature for the detection of Cd2+ ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of PC-CD for Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functionalization of CD was done with PC to detect Cd2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of PC-CD produced ratiometric emission. The Different procedures, such as (1) electron transfer reduction from PC to Cd2+ (2) induced coordination aggregation (3) Forster resonance electron transfer (FRET) between PC and analyte, can be used to explain the PL quenching mechanism of PC-CDs. The FRET mechanism is the primary method by which Cd2+ ion quenches PC in PC-CD solution. A fluorophore donor involving Dipole-dipole interactions upon excitation and acceptor chromophore transfer electrons in a non-radiative manner provides the basis for the FRET phenomenon. Fluorophore functions as an oscillating dipole process that transfers energy to a resonance-bound dipole of similar energy. In FRET research, the chromophore acts as an acceptor and ought to be non-fluorescent. According to FRET, specific requirements for quenching mechanisms must include the following. (i) The PC- CD+ Cd2 should be close to one another (ii) the decrease of PL intensity occurs when the concentration of Cd2+ is added.

Confirming Quenching Studies, time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) fluorescence lifetime study was conducted. TCSPC measurements were made in both the presence and absence of Cd2+ to estimate the fluorescence lifetime of PC-CD ( Fig. 9 a). A bi-exponential fit was used to fit the data, and the decay emission intensity was computed. As a result, Cd2+ does not reduce the fluorescence lifetime of PC-CD, but a quenching process can be seen because of the ground-state interaction of PC-CD with Cd2+. In the presence of Cd2+, the average lifetime was determined to be 12.24 ns, while it was 12.11 ns in the absence of Cd2+. This data suggests that the fluorescence lifespan decreases with the addition of Cd2+, inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism was ruled out [41]. The interaction between the electron-deficient groups of Cd2+ metal ions and the electron-rich nitrogen atoms on the PC-CD is shown by the fluorescence lifetime reduction (FRET) (Scheme 2). FRET is the predominant mechanism developed between PC-CD and Cd2+ metal ion. In addition, Cd2+ is reacted with the synthesized PC-CD and forms a non-fluorescent PC molecule because of the powerful electrostatic contact between the two molecules. Therefore, the FRET from PC-CD to Cd2+ and the development of the nonfluorescent complex between the PC-CD and Cd2+ are the basis for the exceptional selectivity for Cd2+ detection. Stern-Volmer study of the quenching mechanism of PC-CD + Cd2+ has been done to ascertain the mechanism of electron transfer. The Stern-Volmer equation is given as.
(2) F0 / F = 1 + KSV [Q]

KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Q is the concentration of Cd2+ (0–70 µM), and F and F0 characterize the fluorescence intensities of PC-CD in the absence and presence of Cd2+, respectively. Stern-Volmer curvature plot in Fig. 9 b only describes 100% dynamic quenching or static (100%), but not static-dynamic combinations quenching. To obtain a modified straight line against the concentration of Cd2+ with the quenching constant (Kapp) that gives a static-dynamic quenching combination, the Stern-Volmer(SV) equation was modified (Fig. 9 c).
(3) F0 / F = 1 + Kapp[Q]
Where Kapp represents a modified quenching constant.

Using the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay, the viability and cytotoxicity of Hela cells were examined ( Fig. 10). When PC-CDs were added at a varied concentration range (0–100 μg/mL), the cell viability was greater than 96% (24hrs). The outcomes demonstrated the safety and biocompatibility of PC-CD. The synthesized PC-CDs had good biological performance, further supported by their cytotoxicity and fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cd2+ ion in various water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of Cd2+ in water samples from the various industries' effluents was performed using the synthesized PC-CD fluorescent probe. Water samples were collected from nearby enterprises that were produced by different industries and filtered multiple times using a Whatman-filter paper. The water samples were spiked with various Cd2+ concentrations, and the resulting solution was then examined for Cd2+ using the PL method. The range was determined to be 97–100% when the recovery values obtained using the PL methodology were compared to those obtained using the standard method. The Cd2+ in industrial effluents is described in Table 2. We measured the samples' fluorescence intensity ratio (I666/I476) as well as the RGB values of the fluorescence images, once the samples had reached equilibrium. According to the results in Table S6, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for these sample recoveries was 1.05%, and they ranged from 99% to 101%. Furthermore, fluorescence on-site measurements using a smartphone were also carried out. Real samples' analytical findings demonstrated that the on-site method had adequate precision and recoveries in the range of 99–100.54% with an RSD of 1.29% under conditions of high Cd2+ concentration (Table S5). The ratiometric PC-CDs had good practicability for monitoring Cd2+ in real systems, according to the test findings of real samples.

The current work employs a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize highly luminescent CD using water amaranth leaves. For the selective and quantitative detection of Cd2+ion, the fluorescent nanoprobe PC-CD was created by the covalent linkage of CD and pyrene carboxaldehyde (PC). The produced PC-CD can be utilized as a ratiometric nanoprobe to detect Cd2+ with great sensitivity and selectivity because of its outstanding stability, high water solubility, and high quantum yield. The PC-CDs displayed outstanding photostability and were unaffected over a wide range of pH and time. The limit of detection for Cd2+ was 15 nM, with a linear range of 10–70 µM. According to studies, the FRET interaction mechanism happened between PC-CD and Cd2+. The developed ratiometric sensor displayed excellent sensing ability toward the Cd2+ test on industrial samples.

Keerthana P: Conceptualization, Methodology, Avijit Kumar Das: Conceptualization, Bharath M: Characterisation, data interpretation, Munmun Ghosh: Writing – reviewing and editing.Anitha Varghese: Writing – reviewing and editing,

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the centre for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka for providing the required financial support for completing this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.109325.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
